params.text:
Keeping yourself and others safe

De-escalation always comes first, but your personal safety and that of others is never negotiable. Plan for safety before you are in the middle of a difficult situation, and act decisively if the risk rises.

  • Know your exit routes — position yourself so you can leave the room, and never let yourself be backed into a corner.
  • Withdraw when needed — if the risk is rising and de-escalation is not working, calmly remove yourself. Retreating is not failure; it is a sound safety decision.
  • Call for help — summon colleagues, use your alarm or phone, and contact emergency services if there is a serious threat.
  • Protect others — move other people away from the area if it is safe to do so.
  • Never attempt physical restraint unless you are specifically trained and it is a genuine last resort to prevent harm.

params.question: If de-escalation is not working and you feel the risk to your safety is rising, withdrawing from the situation is the right thing to do.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Withdrawing is exactly the right response when risk is rising and de-escalation is not working. Removing yourself and calling for help keeps everyone safer.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Withdrawing to safety and calling for help is a responsible decision, not a failure. Your personal safety must never be compromised.
